name: Hydrogen on Demand
description: Hydrogen on Demand (HOD) refers to the theoretical concept and proposed technologies that generate hydrogen gas only when and where it is needed, eliminating the need for hydrogen storage and transportation. Proponents suggest this approach could overcome many of the safety and logistical challenges associated with a hydrogen-based economy. The core idea revolves around using various chemical reactions or physical processes to liberate hydrogen from a compound (often water) on demand. Several methods have been proposed, including using aluminum alloys reacting with water, chemical hydrides, or specialized electrolysis systems. Some proponents suggest that suppressed or secret technologies exist that allow for highly efficient or even 'free energy' HOD systems. Mainstream science views HOD as a legitimate area of research but emphasizes the importance of energy efficiency and cost-effectiveness. The feasibility of various HOD technologies depends heavily on the energy input required to liberate the hydrogen and the cost of the materials involved. Criticisms often focus on the fact that no HOD system creates energy; they only convert it from one form to another, and the conversion processes often have significant energy losses. Claims of 'free energy' or overly efficient HOD systems are generally considered pseudoscientific and lack credible scientific evidence. The concept gains traction in alternative energy circles due to its potential to solve hydrogen storage problems and its perceived link to suppressed energy technologies.
subtitle: Generating hydrogen only when needed.

claims.evidence: HOD systems generate hydrogen at the point of use, negating the need for storage.
claims.criticism: While true in principle, practical HOD systems still require storage of the hydrogen-containing compound, which can also pose logistical challenges.

claims.claim: HOD can be highly energy efficient, even 'free energy'.
claims.evidence: Claims of innovative HOD technologies exceeding standard energy efficiency limits.
claims.criticism: Violates the laws of thermodynamics. No credible evidence supports 'free energy' claims; all HOD systems require an energy input.

claims.evidence: Anecdotal accounts and claims of patents being bought out to suppress HOD technology.
claims.criticism: Lacks verifiable evidence. Most patents are publicly available, and alleged suppression is often attributed to economic or technical reasons rather than conspiracy.

keyFigures.name: Stanley Meyer
keyFigures.role: Inventor of the 'Water Fuel Cell', a controversial HOD device.

keyFigures.name: David Blume
keyFigures.role: Advocate of using biomass for HOD production.

keyFigures.name: Ulrich Müller
keyFigures.role: Researcher in metal-organic frameworks for hydrogen storage and release.

mythologyConnections: Alchemy's pursuit of transmutation and perpetual motion.
Prometheus's gift of fire, symbolizing energy source acquisition.

timeline.event: Stanley Meyer patents his 'Water Fuel Cell'.
Continued research into various HOD technologies, including metal hydrides and aluminum-water reactions.
Increased interest in HOD due to the growing demand for alternative energy sources.

sources.author: Ballard Power Systems
sources.work: Various publications on hydrogen fuel cell technology
